topic: wet season flooding — roads cut off

turns.content: The roads are cut again. Third time this wet season. I need to work out what supplies we should have stocked before the next one hits.
That sounds stressful, especially if it's been happening repeatedly. How long are you typically cut off when the roads go, and how many people are you stocking supplies for?

Usually 3 to 5 days but last time it was 8 days. There is me and two kids at home, plus the ranger station has supplies for the work crew. The main road in floods at the creek crossing. When that goes under there is no way in or out unless you have a helicopter and we do not.

Eight days is a long stretch. For a household of three with potential for extended isolation, here's a baseline supply list to have ready before each expected flood:

**Food:** Non-perishable staples for at least 10 days — rice, pasta, tinned vegetables, tinned meat/fish, UHT milk, peanut butter, crackers, cereal. Enough drinking water for 10 days (about 30-40 litres minimum, more in the heat).

**Medical:** Any regular medications with at least 2 weeks' buffer, a well-stocked first aid kit, paracetamol, rehydration sachets.

**Power/communication:** Fully charged batteries, torch, satellite phone or radio if you have one, fuel for a generator if applicable.

**Other:** Fuel for vehicles, cooking gas, insect repellent, tarps.

Do you already have most of this covered, or are there gaps?

We have the radio at the station. Generator and fuel. The food is where I fall short. I stock up but the kids go through it faster than I plan for. And fresh food goes off quick up here even in the fridge.

That's a common problem in remote areas. A few strategies for making food last longer:

- **Frozen vegetables** hold up much better than fresh and are just as nutritious — stock the freezer before flood season
- **Powdered milk** as a backup when UHT runs out
- **Dried foods** like lentils, beans, and instant noodles stretch meals out
- **High-calorie snacks** for the kids — muesli bars, dried fruit, nuts — so they're not burning through main supplies as fast
- Keep a written inventory checklist that you tick off before each expected weather event, so you know exactly what's been used since last time

How's your freezer capacity? If you can freeze bread and meat ahead of time, that buys you a lot of extra days.
Freezer is decent size. I do freeze bread already. Good idea on the frozen veg, the kids will not eat tinned peas but they will eat frozen ones. Strange but true.

Kids are like that — texture matters more to them than adults realise. Tinned vegetables go soft and mushy, frozen ones keep their snap. If you can stock up on frozen corn, peas, beans, and broccoli before the wet season peaks, that covers you well.

One other thing worth considering: if you lose power during a flood and the generator runs out, a full freezer stays cold longer than a half-empty one. So packing it tight is actually better for food preservation.

I did not know that about the full freezer. That is proper useful. The power has gone out once during a flood when a tree came down on the line. Took them 4 days to fix it because they could not get a crew in.

Four days without power in that heat is serious. A packed freezer should hold for about 48 hours if you keep the door closed, so that covers about half of it. You might want to keep a couple of bags of ice in the freezer too — they help maintain the cold mass and you can transfer them to an esky to keep essentials cool if the freezer starts warming up.

The esky is a good backup. What about water. We have bore water but sometimes after heavy rain the bore runs muddy for a day or two. I usually have some bottles stored but probably not enough for 8 days.

For three people over 8 days in tropical heat, you'd want at least 50-60 litres of clean drinking water stored. That's roughly 2 litres per person per day for drinking, plus extra for cooking. The easiest approach is to keep a few 10-litre water containers filled and rotated — the kind you can stack. They're cheap and take up less space than bulk bottles.

If the bore runs muddy, you can also get a basic gravity water filter that handles sediment. Not expensive, and useful as a backup for turning bore water drinkable when it's not running clear.

I will look into the filter. The containers I can get next time I am in town. Do you think I should put together a proper checklist I can go through before each wet season, so I am not scrambling every time.

Absolutely. A seasonal preparation checklist would save you a lot of stress. Here's a framework you could adapt:

**Pre-wet season (October-November):**
- Stock freezer and dry goods to 10-day supply
- Fill water containers
- Check generator fuel and oil
- Restock first aid kit and medications
- Charge all backup batteries, check torches
- Check radio is working

**Before each expected weather event:**
- Top up anything that's been used since last stock-up
- Secure loose items outside
- Fill vehicle fuel tanks
- Charge phones

Stick it on the fridge so the kids can help tick things off too. Makes it routine rather than a scramble.

That is practical. I will write one up this weekend while it is fresh in my mind. Living out here you just have to be ready. The country gives you plenty of warning if you know how to read it but the supplies do not stock themselves.

That's a good way to look at it. Preparation is the one part you can fully control. Once the roads go under, you're working with whatever you've got. Getting the checklist done now while you know exactly what you ran short on this time will make next wet season much smoother.
